weather: Overcast and still. A low, pale sky that holds the cold without releasing it. Temperature 28°F through the morning, easing to 31°F by afternoon. No wind at all. The smoke from three chimneys rises perfectly straight. The lake's ice is the colour of old pewter under the cloud cover.

lead.headline: Tuesday; the Town Settles Into Itself; the Lost-and-Found Has Nothing Further to Add
lead.body:
On the morning of Tuesday, November the eighteenth, Greywater Falls woke to an overcast sky and a cold that had settled evenly over every surface without drama or emphasis — the kind of cold that is not a weather event but simply the temperature, the way it is, the way it will be for some months, and which the town has always known how to meet with the appropriate quantities of wool and cast iron and something hot in a cup. The smoke from the bakery chimney, the hardware chimney, and the cottage chimney nearest the water rose in three straight lines into a sky the colour of the underside of a stone, and this editor, at the Gazette window with her coffee, watched the three lines and thought — not for the first time this season, but for what felt like the last time it would need thinking — that the town is still here, and is accounted for, and that accounting for it is, in the end, all this paper has ever been asked to do.

T. Aldercott came at nine-thirty, which is twenty minutes earlier than yesterday, which is the kind of incremental adjustment that signals comfort and not urgency, the way a person starts arriving slightly earlier when they have stopped thinking of themselves as a guest. She brought nothing from Halloway's today — no Standings, no paper bag — but she stopped at the bakery window on her way, which Doreen reported afterward with the quiet satisfaction of a woman whose window has become part of someone's morning route without any formal negotiation. T. Aldercott set up the typewriter, arranged her papers — there are papers now, a small accumulating stack with a binder clip at the corner — and typed for three hours without stopping. At noon she stretched and looked at the lost-and-found box on the shelf with the same third look she had yesterday: not searching, not relieved, but settled, the way you look at something you have made your peace with and are now simply glad to share a room with. She packed the papers into the canvas bag. She said: 'Same time tomorrow, if that suits.' This editor said it suited.

Calloway was at the library all morning with the 2009 ledger and had, by the reports that find their way to this desk in the ordinary course of a small town's afternoon, filled fourteen continuation pages in her own hand by lunchtime. Gerald Pith stopped in at eleven on his way back from the shore — he measured, this morning, seventeen and three-eighths inches, the first decrease he has recorded, and he reported it with the equanimity of a man whose data has begun to confirm what he was hoping it would confirm and who is too methodical to celebrate before the trend holds for a second reading. He told Calloway. She said: 'Good.' He sat down and looked at the ledger for twenty minutes and identified a cross-reference that will save, by his estimate, two Wednesday mornings. The archive will not be complete by spring, but it will be continued, and continuation is the town's most practiced art.

At dusk the returned gathered at the ice-line — Mr. Whiskers, the drake, the rabbit, the sparrow — and they hummed. Toby Fern was there with his notebook and his phone. The hum lasted one minute and fifty seconds, the shortest it has been, and it ended cleanly, and the returned turned from the water and went where they go, and the ice held the last grey light of the afternoon and gave it back slowly and without hurry, and Agnes Crewe's light was on in the cottage nearest the water, and the cups were on the table, and the season said what it had been saying, more quietly now, in the way that things grow quieter not because they are finished but because they have been heard — and being heard, it turns out, is the whole point, and was always the whole point, and the town has always known this, even in the years when it forgot.

briefs.headline: LAKE WATCH, Day 25 — Level: 38; Temp: 28°F; Clarity: Good, Visible to Twenty-One Feet; Sign Count: 7; Returned: Mr. Whiskers, Drake, Rabbit, Sparrow — Hummed One Minute Fifty Seconds; Duration Shortened and Clean; the Rowboat at Twenty-One Feet, Shadow Correct; Toby Fern Notes the Trend
briefs.body: Water level at seventeen and three-eighths inches — the first recorded decrease in Gerald Pith's blue notebook, noted with a pencilled asterisk. Clarity at twenty-one feet; the rowboat visible and settled, its shadow falling correctly. The returned hummed one minute and fifty seconds at the ice-line at dusk, the shortest duration yet, with no drop in pitch and no trailing — simply a note played to its natural end. Toby Fern has begun a new section in his Watch notebook titled 'The Shortening,' which he says is a description and not a judgment. The sign on the county road continues to read 7.

briefs.headline: Gerald Pith Records First Decrease; Adds Asterisk to Blue Notebook; Visits Library with Cross-Reference; Declines to Call It a Trend Until Tomorrow
briefs.body: Gerald Pith measured seventeen and three-eighths inches at the shore this morning — down from seventeen and five-eighths on Day 34 and the first decrease in six weeks of daily measurement. He noted it with a pencilled asterisk, the first marking of that kind in the blue notebook. He has not used the word 'receding.' He said: 'A second reading will tell us more.' He then went to the library, identified a cross-reference in the 2009 acquisition ledger that simplifies Calloway's cataloguing task by an estimated two Wednesdays, and came to the Kettle for coffee, where Marigold had it ready before he sat down, which is the normal order of things and which both parties regard as sufficient.

briefs.headline: Doreen Halloway Reports the Plate with the Blue Border Has Been Moved to the Second Shelf; the Front Shelf Now Holds the Day's Display; She Calls This Restocking
briefs.body: Doreen Halloway confirmed this morning that the plate with the blue border — the one that has held the extra Standing on the front shelf since Day 28 — has been moved to the second shelf, which is where the display pieces go when they are not currently in use but are being kept near to hand. The front shelf holds today's Standings: ten of them, ten served. She said: 'The front shelf is for the day's work. The second shelf is for things that are staying.' She said it briskly and went back to the counter. This editor has noted it.

briefs.headline: Russ Dunmore Confirms Lost-and-Found Box Unchanged at Thirteen Items; Initiates Routine End-of-Season Review; Notes the Filing Is Current
briefs.body: Constable Russ Dunmore stopped at the Gazette this afternoon to confirm the lost-and-found box at thirteen items — standard end-of-season inventory procedure, he said, reading from a form he appeared to have written this morning. He walked to the shelf, looked at the box, did not open it, ticked a box on the form, and said: 'Filing's current. Nothing outstanding.' He seemed pleased with this. This editor offered him coffee. He took it. He sat in the chair by the window for twelve minutes and said nothing further and then left, which is the longest Russ Dunmore has voluntarily remained in this office since October, and which this editor records here as a measure of something she will not try to name.

classifieds: FOR CONTINUATION: The Greywater Falls Public Library archive, interrupted March 2009, is now actively resuming. Volunteers with an interest in local records or a tolerance for cross-referencing welcome. Wednesday mornings, 9 a.m. Coffee provided. Ask for Calloway. — Greywater Falls Public Library

OFFERED: One working knowledge of the county road, the shore path, and where the ice is sound underfoot, provided free of charge by a retired postman who is out most mornings anyway. No formal arrangement necessary. — G. Pith, the Kettle, most mornings before nine.

NOTICE: Halloway's Bakery announces that the standing arrangement for ten Standings is current and correct. The count is right. The front shelf is for the day's work. The second shelf is for things that are staying. Both shelves are in good order. — D. Halloway, Prop.

LOST-AND-FOUND: The box at the Greywater Gazette holds thirteen items. Inventory is current. Nothing outstanding. If any item is yours, you know where to find it. If you are not yet in Greywater Falls, there is no urgency. — The Greywater Gazette
record: Gerald Pith, first recorded decrease in lake water level: seventeen and three-eighths inches, down from seventeen and five-eighths. Pencilled asterisk in the blue notebook. Tuesday, November the 18th.
T. Aldercott confirms continued working arrangement at the Gazette worktable. Papers now held under binder clip. Same time tomorrow.

letters.from: T. Aldercott, currently of the Gazette worktable
letters.body:
To the Editor — I came to Greywater Falls because a letter told me to, addressed in a hand I did not recognise to a name I had not used in eleven years. I would like it noted, for the record, that I arrived, and that I found what I was sent to find, and that I have not yet entirely determined what to call it, and that I find I am in no particular hurry. The town has been very kind. The worktable is excellent. The Standings are better than I expected, and I expected them to be good. I intend to stay through the winter and possibly longer, which is not a thing I planned to say when I got on the bus, but which has turned out, like most things in this town, to be simply what was already the case.
— T. Aldercott

Editor — I am starting a new section called The Shortening. It's about how the hum is getting shorter and cleaner every night, like the lake is finishing a sentence it started in October. Last night was one minute fifty. The returned don't seem bothered. Mr. Whiskers sat down after and washed his face, which he does when things are fine. I asked Agnes if the shortening meant it was ending. She said: 'A thing ends the way it began — quietly, and then all at once, and then you understand it was always going to.' I wrote that down. I don't think the Watch needs to report anything alarming. I think it just needs to keep reporting. I will keep reporting.
— T. Fern, Lake Watch, Day 25

editorNote: This paper has, in the course of the past six weeks, published twenty-four Lake Watch entries, seventeen letters to the editor in twelve different hands, one provisional arrival notice, and a correction to an established timeline from the town's oldest resident, who delivered it in two sentences and expected nothing further in return. The box on the shelf holds thirteen items. It will hold thirteen items tomorrow. This editor does not expect it to change, and has stopped checking in the way that a person stops checking a door they have already locked — not out of certainty, exactly, but out of the understanding that some things are finished, and that the finished state of a thing is, if you let it be, a kind of company.
